evidence_quotes.quote: the large and unapproachable Terms of Service document
the consumer cannot make an informed decision about their purchase of a video game.
clear and visible disclaimer that the publisher is selling it as either a product or a service.

paragraphs.text: Hello, I'm submitting my request for the Digital Fairness Act (DFA) to consider additional laws and regulations for digital products, aiming to protect and preserve digital games that are currently either lacking or underrepresented due to the abuse of legal loopholes. The majority of new video games sold today are no longer given out in a physical format, such as a CD, and instead the box offers a code to access a download of the digital game on the publisher's website or digital marketplaces (Steam, Epic Games Store, GOG, etc.), or the access is directly purchased from said platforms. By having the game only be obtainable through digital means, the publisher has the liberty and ease of integrating online features and functionalities within the game to offer access to their services, which, with this interconnectivity now ingrained in the game, requires it always to have an online/internet connection to the publisher's servers. Due to this "always online" need new games have, when the publisher inevitably decides to no longer maintain and support the game, for not being financially viable or other means beyond their control, the online services shut down, and, because some of the online features and functionalities are integrated with the game's access, the consumer can in no way have access to their product from the lack of a server connection or other service. As it currently stands, publishers have no good practices enforcement and lack repercussions for non-friendly consumer practices as they define their games as a provided service, which will likely end, but at an undefined date or timeframe. Additionally, this information is not readily or clearly available to the average consumer and is instead placed within the large and unapproachable Terms of Service document, usually displayed once the game has been purchased, which means the consumer cannot make an informed decision about their purchase of a video game. With just the perspective and scenario given here, there is a clear need for games to have a clear and visible disclaimer that the publisher is selling it as either a product or a service. The former means you have access to the product and can use it as it is indefinitely, with a method to be able to digitally store the product on a personal storage device and still have access to most of its features, once it is no longer available on the marketplace. While the latter is offered with an estimated support cycle by the publishers, and if the consumer whishes to use the product once official support has ended, the consumer should be able to personally maintain the services that are strictly related to the bare functionality of the game to have it for personal use, non-monetary driven community services, and preserving the art that is presented within this medium. There's a significant percentage of the EU population that cares about this topic and wants better laws and protection for the consumers of digital products and the video game medium as an art form. As an example, the Stop Killing Games movement has achieved almost one and a half million signatures for its European Citizen Initiative petition, so I urge you to consider this issue when further improving the Digital Fairness Act.
